nameMeanings.name: Costello
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Irish Brendan, Declan, John Patrick, Seamus, Siobhan, Conley, Fergus, Liam, Mairead, Sean Patrick.
Irish: shortened Anglicized form of Gaelic Mac Oisdealbhaigh ‘son of Oisdealbhach’, a personal name composed of the elements os ‘deer, fawn’ + dealbhach ‘in the form of, resembling’.
Americanized form of Italian Castello and also of some other surnames beginning with Cast-, for example Castelluccio .

variants.name: Costlow
meanings: Irish: variant of   Costello  .

variants.name: Kostelec
meanings: Slovenian: variant of Kostevc, a habitational name denoting someone from the Kostelsko region in Lower Carniola, named after the castle of Kostel. Compare Costello , Kastelic , and Kostelac .
Croatian: habitational name for someone from Kostel, a place in northern Croatia.

variants.name: Castello
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Spanish Jose, Manuel, Carlos, Juan, Adela, Arturo, Avelino, Dulce, Elena, Emilia, Enrique, Isadora.  Italian Angelo, Antonio, Salvatore, Vito, Aldo, Gaspare, Gaspere, Gino, Giuseppe, Lia, Luigi, Mino.

Italian: from castello ‘castle, fortified building’ (from Late Latin castellum), applied as a topographic name, a habitational name from any of numerous places called with this word, or a metonymic occupational name for a servant who lived and worked in such a place. Compare Costello .

Catalan (Castelló): habitational name from any of the places called Castelló, as for example Castelló de la Plana, one of the main towns in Valencia province (Spain), or Castelló d'Empúries, a town in the Catalan district of L'Empordà.

variants.name: Costley
meanings: Irish (Offaly and Down): variant of   Costello  .

variants.name: Costilow
meanings: Irish: probably a variant of   Costello  .

variants.name: Castelluccio
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Antonio, Domenic, Sal, Salvatore.
Italian: habitational name from any of numerous places called Castelluccio or Castellucchio, from a diminutive of castello ‘castle, fortified building’. Compare Costello .

variants.name: Costelow
meanings: Irish: variant of   Costello  .

nameMeanings.name: Henry
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: A perennially popular given name, of Continental Germanic origin, from haim ‘home’ + rīc ‘power, ruler’. It was an Old French name, adopted by the Normans and introduced by them to Britain. It has been borne by eight kings of England. Not until the 17th century did the form Henry (as opposed to Harry ) become the standard vernacular form, mainly under the influence of the Latin form Henricus and French Henri.

nameMeanings.name: FitzHugh
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English (Northamptonshire): from Anglo-Norman French fi(t)z ‘son’ (see Fitz ) + the personal name Hugh (see Hugh ), ‘son of Hugh’.

History: William Fitzhugh (1651–1701), from Bedford, England, emigrated to VA c. 1670 and established himself on the Potomac River in what was then Stafford County, VA, as a planter and exporter. He also practiced law, was a member of the Virginia House of Burgesses, and served in 1687 as lieutenant colonel of the county militia.

variants.name: Fitz
meanings: Either a short form of any of the numerous names representing a transferred use of a surname formed with the Anglo-Norman French prefix fi(t)z ‘son of ’, as for example Fitzgerald , Fitzroy , or a transferred use of the surname Fitz, which may be of the same derivation, but applied originally to distinguish a son from a father bearing the same given name, or a local name from Fitz in Shropshire, named with the Old English personal name Fitt + hōh ‘hill spur’.

nameMeanings.name: de
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Short form of the various boys' names with the first syllable Ed-, especially Edward .

variants.name: Edward
meanings: From an Old English personal name derived from ēad ‘prosperity, riches’ + weard ‘guard’. This has been one of the most successful of all Old English names, in frequent use from before the Conquest to the present day, and even being exported into other European languages. It was the name of three Anglo-Saxon kings and has been borne by eight kings of England since the Norman Conquest. It is also the name of the youngest son of Queen Elizabeth II . The most influential early bearer was King Edward the Confessor ( ?1002–66 ; ruled 1042–66 ). In a troubled period of English history, he contrived to rule fairly and (for a time at any rate) firmly. But in the latter part of his reign he paid more attention to his religion than to his kingdom. He died childless, and his death sparked off conflicting claims to his throne, which were resolved by the victory of William the Conqueror at the Battle of Hastings. His memory was honoured by Normans and English alike, for his fairness and his piety. Edward's mother was Norman; he had spent part of his youth in Normandy; and William claimed to have been nominated by Edward as his successor. Edward was canonized in the 12th century, and came to be venerated throughout Europe as a model of a Christian king.

en.lifeSummary: Henry FitzHugh de Costello was born about 1224, in Warwickshire, England, United Kingdom. He had at least 1 son with Joane Fourneys.

fr.lifeSummary: Henry FitzHugh de Costello est né environ 1224, à Warwickshire, Angleterre, Royaume-Uni. Il a eu au moins 1 fils avec Joane Fourneys.

de.lifeSummary: Henry FitzHugh de Costello wurde um 1224 in Warwickshire, England, United Kingdom geboren. Er hatte mit Joane Fourneys mindestens 1 Sohn.

it.lifeSummary: Henry FitzHugh de Costello è nato il circa 1224 nel Warwickshire, Inghilterra, Regno Unito. Lui avuto almeno 1 figlio con Joane Fourneys.
